coredata.dc:title: Mint powder assisted synthesis of CQDs/BiOCl with tunable OVs and improved photocatalytic property
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Chengxu
Zhong, Junbo
Chen, Jiufu
Zhu, Yongbing
coredata.dc:description: Development of advanced technologies for the removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) from waterbody has been a challenge because of the persistent, widely distributed and potentially toxic nature of PFOA in the aquatic environment as well as the high binding energy of C-F bond, therefore, it is difficult to remove PFOA by routine heating, ultraviolet radiation and biological degradation. In this study, BiOCl-based photocatalysts containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with visible light reactivity were prepared by a hydrothermal approach using mint powder (MNP) as raw material for CQDs. The prepared samples exhibit self-assembled three dimensions(3D) flower-like microspheres with adjustable oxygen vacancies (OVs). The experimental results show that the activity of BiOCl modified with CQDs is significantly increased compared to that of the reference BiOCl. When the mass ratio of MNP/BiOCl is 2%, the 2% CQDs/BiOCl photocatalyst shows a three-fold enhancement in degradation activity toward destruction of rhodamine (RhB) compared with the reference BiOCl under visible light irradiation. Additionally, degradation activity of 2% CQDs/BiOCl catalyst toward decontamination of PFOA under UV light irradiation also increases. This presentation offers a robust strategy for the synthesis of significantly efficient photocatalysts for detoxification of the organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiOCl
Photocatalytic activity
Carbon quantum dots
OVs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the CQDs/BiOCl (A); the magnified XRD patterns of the (001) crystal plane (B).
SEM images of BiOCl (A); MNP (B); 2% CQDs/BiOCl (C); the magnified figure of 2% CQDs/BiOCl (D); HRTEM image of 2% CQDs/BiOCl (E&F).
Adsorption-desorption isothermal of the samples (A) and the corresponding pores distributions of the samples (B).
EPR patterns of the 0%, 1%, 2% and 3% sample.
Full survey XPS spectra of BiOCl and 2% CQDs/BiOCl (A); C 1 s (B); Bi 4f (C); O 1 s of 0% (D); O 1 s of 2% (E).
FT-IR spectra of the samples prepared.
UV–Vis DRS (A) and bandgap (B) of the samples; Mott-Schottky curves of the photocatalysts (C).
Photocurrent responses of the samples (A); EIS Nyquist plots of the as-prepared samples (B); hydroxyl fluorescence signal of the sample(C).

EPR responses of DMPO-·O2 – (A) and DMPO-·OH (B) for the 2% sample after visible light irradiation for 2 min; Detoxification of RhB on the 2% sample with different trapping agents under visible light, the concentration of scavenger is 5 mmol/L (C); the absorbance of NBT was measured after 40 min under visible shine and the concentration of NBT is 0.675 mmol/L (D).

Decay of RhB on the photocatalysts (A); the fitting curves between ln (C0/Ct) and time (B); UV–Vis spectra evolution of RhB over the 2% sample (C); Degradation of PFOA over the 0% and 2% sample (D); The first-order kinetic curves of PFOA over BiOCl and 2% sample (E).
Five consecutive cycles of RhB degradation over the 2% sample under visible light shine (the total cycle time is 10 h) (A); XRD patterns of the 2% sample for first and fifth recycles (B).
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism of the samples under visible light irradiation.
XPS peak fitting parameters of O 1 s.
Comparison of degradation of PFOA for the Bi-based photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Mint powder assisted synthesis of CQDs/BiOCl with tunable OVs and improved photocatalytic property

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan, School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan
School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Catalysis of Higher Education Institutes of Sichuan, School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of advanced technologies for the removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) from waterbody has been a challenge because of the persistent, widely distributed and potentially toxic nature of PFOA in the aquatic environment as well as the high binding energy of C-F bond, therefore, it is difficult to remove PFOA by routine heating, ultraviolet radiation and biological degradation. In this study, BiOCl-based photocatalysts containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with visible light reactivity were prepared by a hydrothermal approach using mint powder (MNP) as raw material for CQDs. The prepared samples exhibit self-assembled three dimensions(3D) flower-like microspheres with adjustable oxygen vacancies (OVs). The experimental results show that the activity of BiOCl modified with CQDs is significantly increased compared to that of the reference BiOCl. When the mass ratio of MNP/BiOCl is 2%, the 2% CQDs/BiOCl photocatalyst shows a three-fold enhancement in degradation activity toward destruction of rhodamine (RhB) compared with the reference BiOCl under visible light irradiation. Additionally, degradation activity of 2% CQDs/BiOCl catalyst toward decontamination of PFOA under UV light irradiation also increases. This presentation offers a robust strategy for the synthesis of significantly efficient photocatalysts for detoxification of the organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, environmental hazards caused by industrialization have a seriously impact on human society [1–5], among which water pollution seriously threatens our living environment [6–11]. Various industrial wastewaters are discharged into the waterbody along with multifarious organic pollutants, such as rhodamine B [12], tetracycline [13], norfloxacin [14], cefazolin [15] and ciprofloxacin [16], etc. It is difficult for pollutants to degrade spontaneously in nature, and their presence leads to acute threat to ecological cycle, directly threatening human health and even the social economy is also affected by the pollutants [17,18]. Therefore, how to efficiently purify the environment has attracted increasing interest from many researchers. In the existing environmental treatment technology, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has become a hotspot for its high efficiency, green energy saving and safety [19]. Many semiconductor photocatalysts have been extensively studied [20–28]. However, traditional semiconductor catalysts have a series of inherent disadvantages, for instance, wide bandgap, barely response to UV light, low electron-hole pairs separation ability and oxidation ability, which significantly limit their application in actual wastewater treatment. As a result, development of an efficient technology to tackle environmental pollution problems has become an urgent task. Among the current strategies, photocatalytic technology has triggered increasing attention. The key issue of photocatalysis is the development of highly efficient photocatalysts. Amongst the massive photocatalyst, BiOCl has been widely studied owing to its individual layered structure, electronic band structure, non-toxicity, corrosion resistance, distinct optical and electrical properties, and excellent chemical resistance [29–34]. Researchers have affirmed that BiOCl exhibits preferable catalytic performance than TiO2 in destructing of some refractory organic pollutants [35–38]. However, BiOCl demonstrates poor response in visible light region, low photogenerated carriers separation efficiency, and holding low catalytic activity [39–42]. Up to now, many modification approaches have been used to ameliorate the catalytic activity of BiOCl, such as morphology control [43–47], crystal plane exposure [48,49] and surface modification [50–52]. Former studies have affirmed that boosting the catalytic property of BiOCl through construction of heterojunctions is a practical strategy [53,54].

CQDs are nanoscale materials, which show promising application in the field of photocatalysis [55–58]. CQDs have good stability, water solubility and excellent luminescent properties. The electron acceptance and transport properties of CQDs can be used to provide a convenient method for photogenerated electron-hole pairs separation, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and extending the range of light absorption [59–62]. However, conventional preparation methods for CQDs are harsh, energy consumption and costly [63,64]. Compared with other precursors for CQDs, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, Bio-CQDs possess obvious advantages [65,66], for example, good biocompatibility and green.

Mint has some medicinal value as a medicinal plant. Mint can be used to extract essential oils, menthol, and other chemical substances. The active ingredients of mint can be used in anti- corrosion as a green preservative [67]. However, the preparation of CQDs using mint as raw materials have received seldom concern.

In this study, CQDs/BiOCl composite catalysts with tunable OVs and improved photocatalytic performance were synthesized, proven by electron paramagnetic resonance(EPR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and photocatalytic activity tests. The present work can provide some interesting reference for preparation of CQDs/BiOCl composites using green mint as s raw materials for CQDs.
Experimental section was displayed in the Supplementary material.

The prepared catalysts were characterized by XRD, and their phase and composition were studied in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1 A shows that all the photocatalysts are well-crystalized. Diffraction peaks of the samples perfectly coincide with the phase of BiOCl (JCPDS No. 06–0249) [68,69]. Additionally, no characteristic peaks of CQDs were monitored in the XRD patterns, which may due to low loading on CQDs or high dispersion on the surface of BiOCl. The (001) crystalline plane was enlarged and shown in Fig. 1 B. With the increasing of MNP, the characteristic peaks of the (001) crystal planes of all the CQDs/BiOCl samples move to a lower angle compared to the blank BiOCl, suggesting that the crystal spacing becomes larger, leading to OVs, which can be assigned to a strong chemical interaction between BiOCl and CQDs.

To observe morphology of the prepared samples, BiOCl, mint powder, and 2% CQDs/BiOCl were scanned by SEM. Fig. 2
A-Fig. 2 D show morphology of the samples. The result demonstrates that BiOCl holds uneven sheet structures. Fig. 2 B is the SEM of mint powder. It is evident that mint powder shows lump-like appearance. The enlarged vision of 2% CQDs/BiOCl was presented in Fig. 2 C- Fig. 2 D. After introduction of CQDs, the shape of BiOCl is flower-like assembled by large quantities of nanosphere. However, no CQDs can be precisely detected. In comparison to BiOCl, 2% CQDs/BiOCl exhibits a thinner sheet, which is advantageous to boost the efficiency of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs separation from the bulk to the surface. Commonly, thin sheets of BiOCl will induce rich OVs, which can be further ascertained by EPR characterization. Illustrated in Fig. 2 E, some dots can be found on the surface of BiOCl after modification by CQDs. Fig. 2 F shows the HRTEM information for the 2% sample. The presence of CQDs in BiOCl is can be strongly proven, the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm and 0.32 nm is attributed to the (110) crystal plane of BiOCl and the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [41], respectively. The results powerfully support that CQDs/BiOCl heterojunctions have been in-situ constructed.

The samples present type Ⅳ adsorption–desorption isotherms and narrow hysteresis in Fig. 3
A. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 B, the pore sizes are mainly about 10–30 nm, which indicates that the sample has mesoporous structure. Table 1 exhibits the result of BET test. It can affirm that the SBET of CQDs/BiOCl is dramatically higher than that of the blank BiOCl. Generally, more active sites can be generated owing to high SBET, therefore compared to the reference BiOCl, the property of CQDs/BiOCl can be boosted. Additionally, high SBET will definitely influence the separation and transfer efficiency of photoinduced carriers due to the emergence of more defects.

Furthermore, EPR was employed to investigate how CQDs influence the concentration of OVs in the as prepared samples. Fig. 4 demonstrated that all the samples at g = 2.003 show characteristic EPR signal, the presence of OVs is strongly affirmed in the catalyst [70]. As the increasing of CQDs formed, the level of OVs gradually boosts and the 2% sample has the highest level of OVs, then the intensity of EPR peak gradually weakens when the amount of CQDs exceeds 2%. That is to say that the level of OVs is adjustable. CQDs can form C = O bonds on their surface to capture more oxygen atoms from BiOCl nanosheet [71] and generate OVs. OVs play a momentous role in elevating photocatalytic performance and applicable OVs concentration can boost light utilization and photogenerated charge pairs separation efficiency [72]. In addition, OVs also can facilitate the generation of ⋅O2 – [73–76]. Thus, beyond doubt, the samples will demonstrate excellent photocatalytic property.

XPS technique was performed to explore surface chemical state of CQDs/BiOCl. Fig. 5
A shows full peaks on all the surface of composites, Bi, O, Cl and C elements were explored. In the Cl 2p spectrum (Fig. 5 B), there are two peaks with binding energy (B.E.) at 198.7 eV and 198.9 eV, attributing to Cl- [77]. As displayed in Fig. 5 C, the peak with B. E. at 164.8 eV and 159.6 eV can be allocated to Bi 4f5/2 and 4f7/2, individually, which confirms the Bi3+ oxidation state exists in the CQDs/BiOCl [78]. As presented in Fig. 5 D, O 1 s XPS spectra of the prepared samples can be fitted into three peaks, the peak with B. E. at 530.1 eV, 531.6 eV and 533.6 eV can be attributed to lattice oxygen (Bi-O), OVs and the adsorbed oxygen on the surface of composites, separately [79–81]. In Fig. 5 E, the content of OVs on the 2% sample is higher than that on the reference BiOCl. Similarly, as illustrated in Table 2 , the OV content on the 2% CQDs/BiOCl sample is significantly higher than that of the reference sample, which indicates that the 2 % sample will has better photocatalytic property.

To analyze chemical structure for the samples, CQDs and the 2% sample were monitored by FI-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 6 illustrates the absorption peak at 3433 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of –OH. While the absorption peak at 2924 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H. Absorption peak at 1258 cm−1 comes from C-C bond. Additionally, an obvious absorption at 520 cm−1 can be allocated to the vibration of Bi-O [82,83]. Moreover, the characteristic absorption band at 1400 cm−1 appears in both CQDs and the 2% CQDs/BiOCl sample, which is characteristic stretching of the D-bands of CQDs [84]. From the above observations illustrated in Fig. 6, the investigation strongly shows that CQDs have been successfully loaded on BiOCl.

In this study, mint powders were used to prepare CQDs with superior optical properties. As shown in Fig. 7 , optical properties of CQDs were tested with photoluminescence (PL). From Fig. 7, typical PL signal of CQDs at 479.5 nm was detected and no peak was detected in the supernatant of BiOCl. Through the above PL analysis, CQDs can be obtained by a facile hydrothermal reaction using mint as the raw material [85]. Moreover, the signal of CQDs in the system is faint, since CQDs have been loaded onto the surface of BiOCl.

As well known, light absorption property is usually affected by bandgap of a semiconductor. Hence, study the bandgap of a semiconductor has profound significance. UV–Vis spectroscopy is an essential measurement in explore the optical absorption of the catalysts and the test results were depicted in Fig. 8
A. From the results, synthesized BiOCl was investigated with a wavelength range from 300 to 800 nm, and strong absorption peaks of BiOCl can be observed in 300–400 nm. With the amount of CQDs increasing, light absorption capacity of photocatalysts enhances in 400–800 nm. Additionally, optical absorption of the modified photocatalyst is mildly stronger than that of BiOCl in 400–800 nm. As revealed in Fig. 8 B, bandgap (Eg) of the 2% sample and BiOCl is 3.21 eV and 3.33 eV, individually [86]. Valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edge potential of the sample were calculated by an empirical formula. Simultaneously, the flat band potential values and the type of sample were measured by the Mott-Schottky experiment, which is a common approach to test the electrochemical properties of a semiconductor material via a self-assembled electrochemical workstation. The result was shown in Fig. 8 C, which can explain the photocatalytic mechanism. Mott Schottky curve of the composites was observed, and the curve presents a positive slope, demonstrating that the sample is n-type semiconductor [87,88]. Usually, the conduction band edge potential is 0.1–0.3 eV less than the flat-band potential [89,90]. Furthermore, the flat-band potential of BiOCl is −0.25 eV (vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2) based on the intercept of Mott Schottky X-axis. The CB edge potential of BiOCl is approximately −0.35 eV. In view of the CB edge potential and the bandgap, the VB edge potential can be calculated by Eg = EVB-ECB. The VB edge potential of BiOCl is 2.98 eV.

SPS technique was performed to explore the photogenerated charge pairs separation behavior of all the samples. As shown in Fig. 9 , the samples possess violent surface photovoltage response in 320–430 nm and SPS signals of CQDs/BiOCl samples are stronger than that of the blank BiOCl, which indicates that enhanced separation rate of photogenerated carriers can be attained after surface modification of BiOCl by CQDs. It is generally believed that the strong SPS signal benefits from efficient separation rate of photogenerated carriers. In this circumstance, 2% catalyst has the highest SPS signal in 320–430 nm region, simultaneously the photovoltage is about 1.5 μV, which is about 3 times of that of the 0%. As the amount of CQDs further increasing, SPS signal of the sample gradually drops. Nevertheless, excessive CQDs will hinder light penetration the interface and have a remarkable influence on the performance of BiOCl. Fig. 9 shows that under visible light irradiation, CQDs/OVs-BiOCl catalysts produce stronger SPS signal than the blank BiOCl, which indicates that charge pairs can be generated and well separated.

Transient photocurrent (TPR) was used to further test the efficiency that photogenerated charge separation of the prepared samples. Fig. 10
A demonstrates the catalysts exhibit stable photocurrent. As the amount of CQDs increasing, TPR of the sample gradually boosts and then drops as the further elevating of CQDs. TPR of the 2% CQDs/BiOCl sample is strongest, and TPR of the sample is about 0.3 μA, which is about twice of that of the pristine BiOCl. Generally, EIS is performed to study the transfer resistance of the charge pairs generated by the samples. Fig. 10 B displays the smaller radius of the arc, demonstrating enhanced charge pairs transfer capacity. The alternating current impedance radius of the 2% sample is the smallest, exhibiting that the catalyst has the fastest transport rate of photogenerated carriers. From Fig. 10 B, the alternating current impedance radius of the 3% sample is larger than that of the 2% sample, which suggests that superfluous loading of CQDs will hinder the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers. CQDs existing on the surface of BiOCl will induce more defects based on the above characterizations. Therefore, the samples can capture more photogenerated electrons, lowering the recombination rate of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs. Therefore, catalysts will show higher photocurrent density. However, excessive defects will become recombination centers for photogenerated charge pairs, which will reduce the density of the instantaneous photocurrent, thus lowering the photocatalytic performance. Separation of photoinduced carriers can be further studied by fluorescence of hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Coumarin solution was used for testing of relative level of hydroxyl radicals, hydroxyl radicals can produce a weak fluorescence signal with coumarin. Fig. 10 C demonstrates PL of ·OH, PL signal of ·OH produced by the 2% sample is stronger than that generated by the blank sample, confirming that the content of ·OH in the 2% system is higher than the blank BiOCl, revealing high separation rate of the carriers.

After photoexcited carriers are well separated and transferred to the semiconductor surface, photoactivated electrons and holes can induce photocatalytic reactions to form active radicals. The active radicals were investigated by room temperature EPR. As depicted in Fig. 11
A and Fig. 11 B, DMPO-·O2 – and DMPO-·OH responses were collected for BiOCl and 2% photocatalytic reaction system, verifying the presence of reactive oxygen species. Moreover, the EPR responses of DMPO-·O2 – (11A) and DMPO-·OH (11B) in the 2% photocatalytic reaction system with visible light irradiation for 2 min is stronger than those in the BiOCl photocatalytic reaction system, which can be further supported by NBT-·O2 – tests [91,92].

To investigate the role of active radicals in the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl reaction system, capture experiments were depicted in Fig. 11 C. Among the capture agents used, benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO) and isopropanol (IPA) were selected to quench ·O2 –, h+ and ·OH, individually [93,94]. The degradation rate of RhB over the as-prepared photocatalyst is the highest compared with that without the capture agents after visible light shine for 2 h. The decolorization rate of RhB over 2% CQDs/BiOCl is the lowest after adding AO, which is only 14.5 %. Likewise, after adding BQ, the decolorization rate of RhB also drops, and the removal rate is17.6%. From the above results, it can be speculated that holes are the leading active species, and the secondary species are superoxide radicals. In the system, NBT can form a blue precipitate by reacting with ·O2 – generated, which can be used to analyze the level of ·O2 –. Fig. 11 D displays the results of NBT-·O2 – experiments. After UV light irradiation for 40 min, the absorption intensity of NBT at 259 nm obviously decreases in the 0% and 2% photocatalytic reaction system. According to the reaction results, in the photocatalytic system, NBT absorption peak in the 2% system is the lowest. The results show that the 2% system generates more ·O2 –, and higher ·O2 – can improve photocatalytic property by degradation of organic pollutants into smaller molecules.

Fig. 12
A presents that an adsorption–desorption equilibrium can be well established in dark after 30 min, and the adsorption rate of RhB is less than 20%. Fig. 12 A is the result of RhB degradation over CQDs/BiOCl heterojunctions. As can be observed from Fig. 12 A, the degradation efficiency of RhB on the CQDs/BiOCl catalyst is higher than over the blank BiOCl and the CQDs/BiOCl composites display excellent photocatalytic capacity than the pure BiOCl. Among the samples, the 2% sample has the strongest degradation efficiency toward destruction of RhB. However, with the further increasing of CQDs, activity of the sample tents to drop. Photoactivity of the 3% catalyst is lower than that of the 2% catalyst, the low separation efficiency of carriers is one of the reasons. As displayed in Fig. 12 B, destruction of RhB over the samples follow a first-order kinetic. The detoxification rate constant of RhB on the 2% sample is the highest, achieving 0.0252 min−1, whereas the degradation rate constant of RhB over the reference BiOCl is only 0.0063 min−1, and photocatalytic activity of the 2% catalyst is four times of that of the bare BiOCl. Fig. 12 C is the evolution spectrum of RhB over the 2% sample. With reaction time prolonging, the peak gradually decreases and shifts to a lower wavelength, which demonstrates that some intermediates appear, substantially demonstrating the destruction of RhB. After long catalytic reaction time, it is expected that RhB and most intermediates will be completely mineralized to small molecules. Fig. 12 D depicts the degradation efficiency of PFOA on the 2% catalyst with UV light for 2 h is higher than that over the single BiOCl. Moreover, the destruction of PFOA on BiOCl abides by a first-order kinetic, the decontamination rate constant of PFOA on the 0% and the 2% samples is 0.00416 min−1 and 0.00494 min−1, it is clear that photocatalytic property of the 2% catalyst toward removal of PFOA is 1.2 times of that of BiOCl (Fig. 12 E). As illustrated in Table 3 , a comparison was performed between some of Bi-based catalysts reported in the literatures and the CQDs/BiOCl catalysts in this study, and it is apparent that CQDs/BiOCl in this case exhibits excellent photocatalytic performance for degradation of PFOA.

Cycling experiments were carried out to explore the stability of the catalyst by decontamination of RhB over the 2% catalyst with visible light shine. The recycle stability of 2% CQDs/BiOCl was depicted in Fig. 13
A. Photocatalytic property of 2% CQDs/BiOCl slightly decreases after 5 cycles (10 h), which may be due to drop of partial CQDs from the surface of BiOCl. XRD patterns of the 2% catalyst before and after the 5th cycle were employed in Fig. 13 B, which reveals that no significant change appears in the crystallinity and morphology after five cycles. From the above results, the samples still keep a higher photocatalytic activity and have excellent stability, which is favorable to further application in practice.

On account of the experiment results and analysis, photocatalytic mechanism was revealed in Fig. 14 . As shown in Fig. 14, photogenerated e-/h+ pairs will produce by CQD/OVs-BiOCl under light shine. Electrons can directly jump to the energy level induced by OVs, and the photo-activated electrons on the new energy level can further jump to CB of BiOCl. The electrons on the energy level induced by OVs can react with O2 to form ·O2 –, and some of electrons can transfer to CQDs. O2 adsorbed on CQDs can be accelerated to form ·O2 – [84,98] with the participation of electrons. h+ on VB can directly oxidize water or surface –OH to produce ·OH, or directly destruct pollutants. Hence, abundant OVs and CQDs together elevate the separation of carriers in the catalytic system, facilitating the formation of reactive radicals, therefore largely boosting the photocatalytic property of BiOCl.

In this study, CQDs/BiOCl were in-situ fabricated using mint by a routine solvothermal method. Fluorescence spectrum, FT-IR and HRTEM were performed to prove the presence of CQDs. Modification of BiOCl with CQDs can induce abundant defects, which is significant to improve separation of carriers and photocatalytic activity. All the CQDs/BiOCl catalysts demonstrate higher photocatalytic property than BiOCl, the removal efficiency of RhB and PFOA over the 2 % CQDs/BiOCl catalyst is 4 times and 1.2 times higher than that on the reference BiOCl, respectively. Meanwhile, CQDs/BiOCl samples also exhibit high stability in the photocatalytic system. The findings of this work demonstrate a new reliable method to prepare highly efficient adjustable OVs-BiOCl modified with CQDs, and the samples have potential application in remediation of environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (SKLNBC2022-08), Sichuan University of Science and Engineering (2021RC26), City of Zigong (2022ZCYGY08), Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (CSPC201903, CSPC202105), and Chongqing Technology and Business University (KFJJ2022015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2023.07.063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lin, C. Xu, Y. Zhou, S. Liu, W. Liu, A new perspective on volatile halogenated hydrocarbons in Chinese agricultural soils, Sci. Total Environ. 703 (2020) 134646.

J. Gao, B. Jiang, C. Ni, Y. Qi, X. Bi, Enhanced reduction of nitrate by noble metal-free electrocatalysis on P doped three-dimensional Co3O4 cathode: mechanism exploration from both experimental and DFT studies, Chem. Eng. J. 382 (2020) 123034.

J. Gao, B. Jiang, C. Ni, Y. Qi, Y. Zhang, N. Oturan, M. Oturan, Non-precious Co3O4-TiO2/Ti cathode based electrocatalytic nitrate reduction: Preparation, performance and mechanism, Appl. Catal. B Environ. 254 (2019) 391-402.
